my_sentence: For my throat, needs more time to recover. But for my body is no problem.
correction: My throat needs more time to recover. / My body is fine — it's not a problem.
explanation: Starting with "For my X" and then dropping the subject is a Chinese topic-comment pattern. In English the noun itself is the subject: "My throat needs...", "My body is fine." The teacher recast both: "My throat needs more time to recover. It needs more time to recover."
correct_examples: My throat needs more time to recover.
My body is fine; it's not a problem.
context: Recovering from the fever, voice still hoarse.

my_sentence: I eat some food. I eat some pastry and beef.
correction: I ate some food. I ate some pastries and beef.
explanation: The meal already happened, so use the past "ate" — the teacher cut in with "I ate." Also "pastries" should be plural. This eat/ate slip is a recurring one (also corrected on 2026-07-05 and 2026-07-06).
correct_examples: I ate some pastries and beef for dinner.
I ate a lot at lunch today.
context: What he ate before class.

my_sentence: We need to help each other when we meet some problems.
correction: We need to help each other when we encounter some problems.
explanation: "Encounter problems" is the natural collocation; "meet problems" mirrors Chinese 遇到问题. The teacher made the same correction yesterday (2026-07-06) and repeated it today: "when we encounter some problems."
correct_examples: We help each other when we encounter problems.
You may encounter difficulties in a new job.
context: Why people should be considerate.

my_sentence: We can reduce our stress and responsibility.
correction: We can reduce our stress and responsibilities.
explanation: "Responsibilities" needs the plural here (duties are countable and multiple). The teacher also drilled the pronunciation of "stress," which came out garbled as "stressed / restressed."
correct_examples: Sharing chores reduces our stress and responsibilities.
He has many responsibilities at work.
context: Benefits of considerate roommates.

my_sentence: We want to have a good mood, so we need to have a good mood, so we need to find...
correction: We have to keep a good mood, so we must find some open-minded roommates.
explanation: The teacher's big rule of the day: "Stop repeating verbs, nouns, adjectives and adverbs in your sentences." When a word already appeared, switch to a synonym (have → keep, need → have to / must) or restructure. She stopped the class to write this reminder down after "if you want to become a doctor... if you want to become a nurse."
correct_examples: We have to keep a good mood, so we must find easygoing roommates.
If you want to become a doctor you need a high score; to be a nurse, you don't.
context: Repeated words within one sentence — corrected in sessions 1 and 2.

my_sentence: We have to find a pretty roommates.
correction: We have to find some good-natured or open-minded roommates.
explanation: "Pretty" describes appearance, but the point was character, so the teacher supplied "good-natured / open-minded." Also "a" cannot go with the plural "roommates" — use "some."
correct_examples: We have to find some open-minded roommates.
She's looking for a good-natured person to live with.
context: What kind of roommates to look for.

my_sentence: That can help us keep our energy and pay patient.
correction: That can help us keep our energy and patience.
explanation: The noun is "patience"; "patient" is the adjective (or a person in a hospital). Same noun/adjective pair issue as silent/silence from an earlier class. The teacher drilled: "Patience. Patience."
correct_examples: Good roommates help us keep our energy and patience.
You need a lot of patience to teach children.
context: Benefits of considerate roommates.

my_sentence: It can help you and your neighbor keep a good relationship.
correction: It can help you and your neighbors keep a good relationship with each other.
explanation: The teacher corrected the singular to "neighbors" (you have more than one) and completed the phrase with "with each other."
correct_examples: Being considerate helps you and your neighbors keep a good relationship with each other.
We get along well with our neighbors.
context: Why being considerate is a good thing.

my_sentence: I think for most of people are willing to do that.
correction: I think most people are willing to do it.
explanation: "Most people" takes no "of" (and no leading "for") unless a determiner follows: "most of THE people." The teacher's recast: "I think most people are willing to do it."
correct_examples: Most people are willing to help.
Most of the people in my team are considerate.
context: Whether people are willing to be considerate.

my_sentence: I have met a lot of considerate people in my neighborhood, company and school.
correction: I have met a lot of considerate people in my neighborhood, in the company and at school.
explanation: Each place takes its own preposition pattern: "in my neighborhood," "in the company," "at school." The teacher modeled the full string.
correct_examples: I've met considerate people in my neighborhood, in the company and at school.
She works at school and lives in my neighborhood.
context: How many considerate people he has met.

my_sentence: For me, I'm all focused on fixing problems.
correction: For me, I am more focused on fixing problems.
explanation: "More" came out sounding like "all." The teacher corrected it twice: "For me, I am more focused on." Watch the /m/ + /ɔːr/ in "more."
correct_examples: I'm more focused on solving problems than chatting.
She's more interested in design than coding.
context: Why he is not a talkative person.

my_sentence: I'm more focused on fixing problems or thinking some questions, not talk with the other.
correction: I'm more focused on fixing problems or thinking about some questions, not talking with others.
explanation: After "focused on," all the parallel items stay in the -ing form: fixing... thinking... not talking. Also "others" (plural pronoun), not "the other," and "think ABOUT a question."
correct_examples: I'm focused on fixing bugs, not talking with others.
He spends his time reading, not watching TV.
context: Why he is not talkative at work.

my_sentence: I like to thinking.
correction: I like to think.
explanation: After "to," use the base form: "to think." Either "I like to think" or "I like thinking" is fine — mixing them ("to thinking") is not.
correct_examples: I like to think through problems by myself.
I like thinking about hard questions.
context: Explaining his quiet working style.

my_sentence: If I hard work, I can finish project quickly.
correction: If I work hard, I can finish my projects quickly.
explanation: As a verb phrase the order is "work hard" (verb + adverb); "hard work" is the noun. The teacher: "If I work hard." She also added the missing determiner: "my projects."
correct_examples: If I work hard, I can finish my projects quickly.
Hard work pays off.
context: Why a project partner should be hardworking.

my_sentence: I will have enough time to prepare.
correction: I can have enough time to prepare.
explanation: The teacher again softened "will" to "can" — it's a possibility, not a certainty. Same will→can correction as yesterday (2026-07-06).
correct_examples: If I finish early, I can have enough time to prepare.
You can run into delays, so leave a buffer.
context: Benefits of working hard on a project.

my_sentence: I can have enough time to prepare something for them.
correction: I can have enough time to prepare my own projects.
explanation: "Them" had no clear referent — the teacher asked "who is them?" A pronoun needs an antecedent the listener already knows; otherwise name the thing: "my own projects."
correct_examples: I can have enough time to prepare my own projects.
I finished early, so I prepared materials for the next task.
context: What he does with time saved by working hard.

my_sentence: It will affect other people who cooperate with us.
correction: It will affect other people who cooperated with us.
explanation: The teacher put the relative clause in the past — "who cooperated with us" — matching the people who already joined the project.
correct_examples: Missing the deadline will affect the people who cooperated with us.
We thanked everyone who helped us.
context: Consequences of not finishing a project on time.

my_sentence: I didn't want to make some problems for them.
correction: I just don't want to trouble them.
explanation: "Make some problems for someone" is unnatural; the teacher offered the verb "trouble": "you just don't want to trouble them." Also the feeling is current, so present "don't," not "didn't."
correct_examples: I just don't want to trouble them.
Don't trouble him with small questions.
context: Why he works hard for his teammates.

my_sentence: A lot of people do that every day.
correction: A lot of people do those things every day.
explanation: The referent was plural (wasting others' time, troubling them...), so the teacher corrected to "do those things." Singular "that" points to one thing only.
correct_examples: A lot of people do those things every day.
Those habits are hard to change.
context: People who waste others' time.

my_sentence: I think there are a lot of regions for them to do that.
correction: I think there are a lot of reasons for them to do that.
explanation: "Reasons" /ˈriːzənz/ came out as "regions" /ˈriːdʒənz/ — the difference is the /z/ vs /dʒ/ in the middle. The teacher: "Reasons, I should say."
correct_examples: There are a lot of reasons for the delay.
This region of China is famous for tea.
context: Why some people can't finish tasks on time.

my_sentence: Our evaluation depend on expert.
correction: Our evaluations depend on the expertise.
explanation: "Expertise" (the skill) is the right word here, not "expert" (the person) — the teacher: "not on the experts, but the expertise... the masterful skills of people." Also plural subject + verb agreement: "evaluations depend."
correct_examples: Our evaluations depend on the expertise of the team.
She is an expert; her expertise is in networking.
context: How project evaluations work at his company.

my_sentence: If you want to succeed, hardworking is necessary.
correction: If you want to succeed, hard work is necessary.
explanation: "Hardworking" is an adjective (a hardworking person); the noun is "hard work." The teacher: "Hardworking is an adjective. Do not make it a noun. Hard work is the proper way."
correct_examples: Hard work is necessary if you want to succeed.
She is a hardworking student.
context: Whether hard work guarantees success.

my_sentence: If you do something in right places and right time...
correction: If you do something at the right place and time...
explanation: The set phrase is "AT the right place and (at the right) time," with the article "the." The teacher corrected both the preposition and the missing article.
correct_examples: Success often means being at the right place at the right time.
He was at the right place and time to get the job.
context: Luck as a factor in success.

my_sentence: If you invested in mobile phones 20 years ago, you can get succeed easily.
correction: You can succeed easily.
explanation: "Succeed" is already a verb, so it follows "can" directly — no "get." The teacher: "Succeed is a verb... if you know it has a verb form, do not try to use it as a noun." (Same rule as "help," "promote.")
correct_examples: If you had invested in mobile phones then, you could succeed easily.
Work smart and you can succeed.
context: Being early in the mobile-phone era.

my_sentence: There are too many companies provide smart devices.
correction: There are too many companies that provide smart devices.
explanation: Two verbs ("are" and "provide") need a connector: the subject relative pronoun "that." The teacher stopped him immediately: "That, that — relative pronoun. There are too many companies THAT..."
correct_examples: There are too many companies that provide smart devices.
There are many apps that do the same thing.
context: Why entering the mobile market now is too late.

my_sentence: One of my friends has been learning new skills about website for many years.
correction: One of my friends has been learning new skills about websites for many years.
explanation: Talking about websites in general takes the bare plural "websites," not singular "website" with no article. The teacher: "About websites? Websites."
correct_examples: He has been learning skills about websites for years.
She builds websites for small companies.
context: A friend whose hard work hasn't paid off.

my_sentence: If you make a big contributor, you can get promoted.
correction: If you make a big contribution, you can get promoted.
explanation: "Contribution" is the thing you make; "contributor" is the person who makes it. The teacher: "Contribution."
correct_examples: If you make a big contribution, you can get promoted.
He is a major contributor to the open-source project.
context: What promotions depend on.

my_sentence: If your manager wants to give you some promotion...
correction: If your manager promotes you...
explanation: Same verb-over-noun rule: "promote" is a verb, so use it directly instead of "give you some promotion." The teacher: "If your manager promotes you. Verb."
correct_examples: If your manager promotes you, your salary rises.
She was promoted last year.
context: How promotions work at his company.

my_sentence: Your projects can finished and your skills are both important for your promotion.
correction: Your projects can be finished, and your skills are both important for your promotion.
explanation: The passive needs "be": "can BE finished." The teacher supplied it: "Your projects can... can be finished."
correct_examples: Whether your projects can be finished on time matters for promotion.
The report can be completed by Friday.
context: What managers check before promoting someone.

my_sentence: You will have more time to learn new things, or share your knowledge to other.
correction: You will have more time to learn new things, or share your knowledge with others.
explanation: (assistant note) "Share" goes with "with": share something WITH someone. And the plural pronoun is "others." The teacher didn't stop him here, but corrected the same "others" form elsewhere.
correct_examples: I share my knowledge with others on the team.
She shared her notes with us.
context: Benefits of learning to use AI at work.

my_sentence: A nurse salary is about 5000 to 10000 per month.
correction: A nurse's salary is about 5,000 to 10,000 yuan per month.
explanation: The salary belongs to the nurse, so it needs the possessive 's: "a nurse's salary." The teacher corrected it twice: "A nurse's salary."
correct_examples: A nurse's salary in my country is about 5,000 to 10,000 yuan a month.
A doctor's salary is much higher.
context: Comparing nurse salaries in China and the Philippines.

my_sentence: If you want to become a doctor, you need to get high score.
correction: If you want to become a doctor, you need to get a high score.
explanation: "Score" is a singular countable noun, so it needs "a": "a high score." The teacher corrected this three times in a row ("A high score") until it stuck.
correct_examples: You need to get a high score to become a doctor.
She got a high score on the entrance exam.
context: China's college entrance exam and medical school.

my_sentence: I don't think our country export nurses to other countries.
correction: I don't think our country has exported nurses to other countries.
explanation: Talking about whether something has ever happened takes the present perfect: "has exported." The teacher prompted "Exported" and he self-corrected mid-sentence.
correct_examples: Our country has never exported nurses.
Has your country ever produced famous scientists?
context: Whether China exports nurses abroad.

my_sentence: If you want to become a doctor, you can choose medicine college.
correction: You can choose to learn medicine in college.
explanation: "Choose" here takes an infinitive: "choose TO LEARN medicine," and the place is "in college." "Choose medicine college" jams the noun phrases together.
correct_examples: You can choose to learn medicine in college.
He chose to study computer science.
context: Becoming a doctor via the college entrance exam.

my_sentence: I find there are a lot of companies that recruit DevOps engineers. I checked there requirements. I find it's not difficult for me.
correction: I found that there are a lot of companies recruiting DevOps engineers. I checked their requirements and found they are not difficult for me.
explanation: The discovering already happened, so "found (that)," matching "checked." The teacher: "I found that." Note also "their requirements" (possessive), which the transcript garbled as "there."
correct_examples: I found that many companies are recruiting DevOps engineers.
I checked their requirements and found them reasonable.
context: Job prospects abroad as a computer engineer.

my_sentence: That's 90% possibility I can find a suitable job in Uk.
correction: There's a 90% possibility I can find a suitable job in the UK.
explanation: "Possibility" is a singular countable noun here, so it needs "a"; and country names with initials take "the": "the UK." The teacher echoed "in the UK."
correct_examples: There's a 90% possibility I can find a suitable job in the UK.
She moved to the UK last year.
context: Estimating his chances of being hired abroad.

my_sentence: If you cooperate with someone it talkative, you can easily get and share information.
correction: If you cooperate with someone who is talkative, you can easily get and share information.
explanation: An adjective after "someone" needs "who is": "someone who is talkative." The teacher corrected this same pattern yesterday ("someone who is easygoing") and flagged it again twice today ("Someone who is talkative"; "your relative pronoun, go on").
correct_examples: I like working with someone who is talkative.
There are a lot of people who like to talk in my company.
context: Why a talkative project partner is useful.

my_sentence: You can listen to a lot of stories or gossip from him or her. You can get some information from it.
correction: You can get some information from him or her.
explanation: The source is a person, so the pronoun is "him or her," not "it." The teacher: "From it? Ok — from him or her."
correct_examples: You can get a lot of information from him or her.
I heard the news from her.
context: Talkative coworkers as an information source.

my_sentence: I think for me a considerate is the most important.
correction: I think a considerate person is the most important for me.
explanation: "Considerate" is an adjective and can't stand alone after "a" — it needs the noun: "a considerate person." Same pattern as "an easygoing person" from 2026-07-06.
correct_examples: A considerate person is the most important to me.
I'd rather live with a considerate person.
context: Which quality matters most to him.

my_sentence: If you don't like something that he or she did, you just need to tell him or her.
correction: If you don't like something a person does, you just need to tell him or her.
explanation: "He or she" appeared with no antecedent — the teacher: "you don't have a precedent [antecedent], so you must not use he or she." Introduce the person first ("a person"), then pronouns can follow. She also fixed "did" to the general-truth present "does."
correct_examples: If you don't like something a person does, just tell him or her.
When a roommate is noisy, tell him or her directly.
context: Handling problems with a considerate person.

my_sentence: I'd prefer someone who is fairly independent and don't trouble me.
correction: I'd prefer someone who is fairly independent and doesn't trouble me.
explanation: "Someone" is singular, so it takes "doesn't" / "does not." The teacher corrected this three times across sessions ("And doesn't, sir", "And does not ask", "Asks") and stated the rule: "always check if your main subject is one or many."
correct_examples: I'd prefer someone who is independent and doesn't trouble me.
A fairly independent person finishes his or her tasks and does not ask others for help.
context: Preferred travel companion; repeated in session 3.

my_sentence: If he or she always asks me, it will bother me.
correction: If he or she asks me repeatedly, that will bother me.
explanation: The teacher upgraded "always asks" to the more precise "asks repeatedly": "If he or she asks me repeatedly, you should say."
correct_examples: If someone asks me repeatedly, it bothers me.
He repeatedly asked the same question.
context: When asking for help becomes annoying.

my_sentence: A complaint is something that we don't comfortable.
correction: A complaint is something that we are not comfortable with.
explanation: "Comfortable" is an adjective, so it needs "be": "we ARE not comfortable," and the clause needs the ending preposition "with." The teacher: "Comfortable is an adjective" and later "comfortable with."
correct_examples: A complaint is about something we are not comfortable with.
I'm not comfortable with loud music at night.
context: Defining the word "complaint".

my_sentence: The neighbor must complaint to...
correction: The neighbor complains about the noise. / The neighbor's complaint is about the noise.
explanation: "Complain" (no T) is the verb; "complaint" (with T) is the noun — the thing you say. The teacher spelled it out: "Complaint is the noun form, complain is the verb form. Can you see the difference?"
correct_examples: The neighbor is complaining about the loud noise.
They filed a complaint about the karaoke at night.
context: Vocabulary work on complain vs. complaint.

my_sentence: There are too much cabbage on the street.
correction: There's too much garbage on the street.
explanation: "Garbage" /ˈɡɑːrbɪdʒ/ came out as "cabbage" /ˈkæbɪdʒ/ — the initial /ɡɑːr/ vs /kæ/ is the difference. The teacher confirmed the intended sentence: "There's too much garbage on the street."
correct_examples: There's too much garbage on the street.
We bought a cabbage at the market.
context: Giving a complaint about the world.

my_sentence: Our temperature, it shall become higher and higher.
correction: Our temperature is becoming higher and higher.
explanation: An ongoing trend takes the present continuous: "is becoming." "Shall become" is archaic/wrong here, and the doubled subject ("our temperature, it") should be dropped.
correct_examples: The temperature is becoming higher and higher.
Summers are getting hotter every year.
context: Complaints about the world: global warming.

my_sentence: Like cars and air conditions and other things.
correction: Like cars and air conditioners and other things.
explanation: The machine is an "air conditioner"; "air conditioning" is the system/process. "Air conditions" is neither. The teacher: "Air conditioners."
correct_examples: We bought two air conditioners for the apartment.
The air conditioning in the office is too cold.
context: Devices that consume resources.

my_sentence: We are using more and more resources, so our temperature is increasing.
correction: We are using more and more resources, so our temperature is rising.
explanation: For temperature the natural verb is "rise": "the temperature is rising." The teacher: "Not increasing, but mostly rising."
correct_examples: The temperature is rising every year.
Prices are rising quickly.
context: Why the world is getting hotter.

my_sentence: Whenever I take their card, I can smell something bad in it.
correction: Whenever I ride their car, I can smell something bad in it.
explanation: The teacher corrected to "ride their car" — and note the pronunciation: "car" /kɑːr/ kept coming out as "card" throughout the session. (You "take a taxi" as a whole, but with "their car" the verb is "ride.")
correct_examples: Whenever I ride in a taxi, I check the seat first.
I take a taxi to work every day.
context: His complaint that taxis are dirty and smelly.

my_sentence: I want to find a new car to instead of it.
correction: I want to find another car instead of the dirty one.
explanation: "Instead of" is a preposition phrase — it takes a noun or -ing form and never follows "to": "another car instead of the dirty one," not "to instead." The teacher: "To plus verb... instead of what? What is the it?" — also name the thing instead of a vague "it."
correct_examples: I found another taxi instead of the dirty one.
We walked instead of taking a bus.
context: What he does when a taxi is dirty.

my_sentence: Some of taxi drivers like to smoke.
correction: Some taxi drivers like to smoke. / Some of the taxi drivers like to smoke.
explanation: (assistant note) "Some of" must be followed by a determiner ("some of THE taxi drivers"); with a bare plural noun, drop "of": "some taxi drivers." Same rule as "most of people" → "most people" from session 1.
correct_examples: Some taxi drivers like to smoke.
Some of the drivers I met were very polite.
context: Complaints about taxi drivers smoking.

my_sentence: Our market complete very serious.
correction: Our market competition is extremely fierce.
explanation: The intended noun is "competition" (not "complete"), it needs the verb "is," and the natural intensity word for competition is "fierce" (not "serious"). The full corrected sentence he produced: "Our market competition is extremely fierce, so they gave it up."
correct_examples: Market competition in China is extremely fierce.
Competition for that job was fierce.
context: Why Uber and Grab left the Chinese market.

my_sentence: At the beginning, you can ride a taxi for free.
correction: In the beginning, you could ride a taxi for free.
explanation: Standing alone, the phrase is "IN the beginning"; "at the beginning" needs an "of" phrase ("at the beginning of the year"). The teacher: "In the beginning." Past context also wants "could."
correct_examples: In the beginning, rides were free.
At the beginning of the promotion, taxis were free.
context: The ride-hailing subsidy war in China.

my_sentence: Taxi drivers can get double of your share.
correction: Taxi drivers can get double the payment.
explanation: The money a passenger pays is "the payment" (or "the fare"), not "your share," and the pattern is "double THE payment" (no "of"). The teacher: "The pay... of the payment."
correct_examples: Drivers could get double the payment from the company.
They charged double the normal fare.
context: How the subsidy war paid drivers.

my_sentence: In my country, the taxi fee depend on your mile.
correction: In my country, the taxi fee depends on the distance.
explanation: "Distance" is the word for how far you travel ("mile" is just one unit, and unclear here); the teacher accepted his rephrase "based on your distance." Also singular subject agreement: "the taxi fee dependS."
correct_examples: The taxi fee depends on the distance.
You pay based on the distance you travel.
context: Why Chinese airport taxis don't overcharge.

my_sentence: When you use Didi or Uber, you can get the distance when you open it.
correction: You can check the distance when you open the app.
explanation: You "check" information on an app — the teacher insisted twice: "You can check, not get."
correct_examples: You can check the distance on the app before the ride.
I checked the route on my phone.
context: How GPS apps prevent taxi-driver detours.

my_sentence: Their taxi fees are more than five to ten times than us.
correction: Their taxi fees are five to ten times higher than ours.
explanation: A multiplier needs a comparative adjective: "five to ten times HIGHER than." And the comparison is with "ours" (our fees), not "us" (the people). The teacher: "Are higher — five to ten times higher than ours."
correct_examples: Taxi fees in Japan are five to ten times higher than ours.
Rent there is three times higher than in my city.
context: Comparing taxi prices in Japan and China.

my_sentence: I remembered that I ride a taxi from airport to the company. It's only 20min and spent more than 5000.
correction: I rode a taxi from the airport to the company. It was only 20 minutes, and it cost me more than 5,000.
explanation: A past anecdote needs past forms throughout: "rode," "was," and "it COST me" (money costs you; you don't "spend" without a subject). The teacher fed each piece: "I rode a taxi," "from the airport to the," "it was only," "it cost me."
correct_examples: I rode a taxi from the airport to the company; it cost me 5,000 yen.
The trip was only 20 minutes but cost a lot.
context: An expensive taxi ride in Japan.
